Name: task_aware_rate_distortion_quantization
Title: Task-Aware Rate-Distortion Bounds for Neural Network Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By quantifying the mutual information between the task and the quantized representation, we can derive task-aware rate-distortion bounds that optimize bit allocation based on task relevance, leading to more efficient compression without sacrificing task performance.
Related Work: Existing quantization methods (e.g., EMQ, AMP-ViT) focus on minimizing distortion metrics like MSE without considering task-specific sufficiency. Recent work on rate-distortion bounds for generalization (e.g., Sefidgaran et al., 2022) provides a theoretical foundation but does not apply it to quantization. This proposal distinguishes itself by integrating task-aware mutual information into quantization, enabling optimal bit allocation based on task relevance.
Abstract: Neural network quantization is a key technique for model compression, but existing methods often optimize for fixed distortion measures without considering task-specific sufficiency. We propose a novel framework that derives task-aware rate-distortion bounds by quantifying the mutual information between the task and the quantized representation. Our approach enables optimal bit allocation based on task relevance, ensuring that the compressed representation preserves task-critical information while minimizing redundancy. We derive theoretical bounds and propose an efficient algorithm for task-aware quantization. Experiments on vision and language tasks demonstrate that our method achieves superior compression efficiency and task performance compared to existing quantization methods.
Experiments: Derive task-aware rate-distortion bounds for quantization using mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE).
Develop an algorithm for task-aware bit allocation based on the derived bounds.

Evaluate on vision (ImageNet) and language (GLUE) tasks, comparing compression efficiency (bits-per-weight) and task performance (accuracy/F1) against state-of-the-art quantization methods (e.g., EMQ, AMP-ViT).
Analyze the trade-off between compression ratio and task performance for different bit allocation strategies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating mutual information between the task and the quantized representation may be computationally expensive.
The derived bounds may not be tight for all tasks or architectures.
The algorithm may require task-specific fine-tuning for optimal performance.
